title: Composting Cardboard and Paper Safely
excerpt: Cardboard and paper can be excellent brown materials for compost when they are plain, uncoated, and used in moderation. Knowing which types are safe and how to prepare them makes it easier to balance wet scraps and build a cleaner, more effective compost pile.
author: Permatable Team
content:
Brown Materials Guide

Cardboard and paper can be excellent compost ingredients when used the right way. They are easy to find, help balance wet food scraps, and add carbon to the compost pile. For many home composters, they are some of the most useful brown materials available.

But not every type of paper or cardboard belongs in compost. Some are safe and helpful, while others contain coatings, inks, or synthetic materials that should stay out. Knowing the difference helps you compost these materials safely and effectively.

Why Cardboard and Paper Are Useful in Compost

Most compost piles need a mix of green and brown materials. Greens are rich in nitrogen and include things like food scraps and grass clippings. Browns are rich in carbon and include dry materials like leaves, straw, cardboard, and paper.

Cardboard and paper help because they:

  • Add carbon
  • Absorb extra moisture
  • Improve pile structure
  • Reduce odor from wet scraps
  • Make use of common household waste

If your compost tends to get soggy, cardboard and paper can be especially helpful.

Are Cardboard and Paper Browns or Greens?

Cardboard and most plain paper are considered brown materials in compost.

That means they are best used to balance wetter, nitrogen-rich ingredients such as:

  • Fruit and vegetable scraps
  • Coffee grounds
  • Tea leaves
  • Fresh grass clippings
  • Plant trimmings

A healthy compost pile often needs more browns than greens, so cardboard and paper can play an important role.

What Types of Cardboard Are Safe to Compost?

Many kinds of plain cardboard are safe and useful in compost.

Usually safe options include:

  • Plain corrugated cardboard
  • Brown shipping boxes
  • Toilet paper rolls
  • Paper towel rolls
  • Plain cardboard egg cartons
  • Non-glossy food boxes without plastic lining

The safest cardboard is simple, uncoated, and free of shiny surfaces.

What Types of Paper Are Safe to Compost?

Many everyday paper products can also be composted safely.

Usually safe paper includes:

  • Newspaper
  • Plain printer paper in moderation
  • Shredded office paper without heavy coatings
  • Paper napkins
  • Paper towels used for food or water
  • Uncoated paper bags
  • Paper egg cartons

These materials break down well, especially when shredded or torn into smaller pieces.

What Cardboard and Paper Should You Avoid?

Not all paper-based materials are compost-friendly.

Avoid adding:

  • Glossy magazines
  • Laminated paper
  • Plastic-coated cardboard
  • Wax-coated cardboard
  • Heavily colored or metallic paper
  • Receipts with thermal coatings
  • Paper with glitter or foil
  • Takeout containers with plastic lining
  • Stickers, tape, and labels

These materials may contain synthetic substances or coatings that do not break down well in a home compost pile.

Remove Tape, Labels, and Stickers First

Even safe cardboard often comes with parts that should not go into compost.

Before composting cardboard, remove:

  • Plastic tape
  • Shipping labels
  • Stickers
  • Staples if possible
  • Synthetic packing materials

Small amounts of tape are sometimes overlooked, but it is better to remove as much as you can so the finished compost stays cleaner.

Is Ink a Problem?

This is a common concern, but most modern newspaper and cardboard inks are generally considered much safer than older inks.

In many cases, black and standard printing inks on plain paper and cardboard are acceptable in home compost. Still, it is best to avoid:

  • Glossy colored inserts
  • Metallic print
  • Thick coated packaging
  • Heavily dyed decorative paper

Plain, simple materials are always the safest choice.

Why Shredding Helps

Cardboard and paper break down much faster when they are torn or shredded first.

Shredding helps because it:

  • Increases surface area
  • Prevents large pieces from matting together
  • Makes mixing easier
  • Improves moisture balance
  • Speeds decomposition

Large sheets of cardboard can take a long time to break down if left whole. Smaller pieces work much better in most home compost systems.

How to Prepare Cardboard for Compost

A simple preparation method works well:

  1. Remove tape, labels, and plastic parts.
  2. Tear or shred the cardboard into smaller pieces.
  3. Wet it lightly if it is very dry.
  4. Mix it with greens in the pile.

Corrugated cardboard is especially useful once shredded because it adds both carbon and some airflow structure.

How to Use Paper and Cardboard in the Compost Pile

The best way to compost paper and cardboard is to mix them with wetter materials.

For example:

  • Kitchen scraps + shredded cardboard
  • Coffee grounds + torn newspaper
  • Grass clippings + paper towels
  • Fruit peels + brown paper

This keeps the pile balanced and helps prevent soggy, smelly conditions.

Do Not Add Too Much at Once

Paper and cardboard are useful, but too much can slow the pile down.

If added in large dry masses, they can:

  • Absorb too much moisture
  • Clump together
  • Limit airflow
  • Slow decomposition

They work best when mixed in gradually rather than added as one huge layer.

Should You Wet Cardboard First?

Dry cardboard can be slow to break down, so moistening it slightly before adding it can help.

This is especially useful if:

  • The pile is already dry
  • The cardboard is thick
  • The weather is hot
  • You are adding a lot at once

It should be damp, not soaking wet. The goal is to help it integrate into the pile more easily.

Is Shredded Office Paper Safe?

Shredded office paper is usually fine in moderation, especially if it is plain and non-glossy.

However, very fine shredded paper can mat together if used heavily. To avoid that:

  • Mix it with coarser materials
  • Combine it with leaves or cardboard
  • Avoid adding dense clumps

Used carefully, it can be a helpful brown material.

Can You Compost Paper Towels and Napkins?

Yes, many paper towels and napkins can be composted if they were used for simple, compost-safe purposes.

Usually fine:

  • Towels used to dry hands
  • Napkins with food crumbs
  • Paper used for water spills
  • Plain uncoated napkins

Better avoided:

  • Towels soaked with chemicals
  • Oily or greasy cleanup paper
  • Heavily scented products

Think about what is on the paper, not just the paper itself.

Cardboard and Paper for Odor Control

One of the best reasons to keep cardboard and paper nearby is that they help control compost odor.

Because they absorb moisture and cover fresh food scraps, they can:

  • Reduce smell
  • Discourage flies
  • Improve balance
  • Keep the pile tidier

A little shredded cardboard added regularly can make composting much easier.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

A few mistakes can make cardboard and paper less effective.

  • Using glossy or coated materials: these do not break down well and may contain unwanted substances
  • Leaving cardboard in large sheets: big pieces decompose slowly and can form barriers in the pile
  • Adding too much dry material at once: this can make the compost too dry or slow
  • Forgetting to remove tape: plastic residues can end up in finished compost
  • Using paper to replace all other browns: variety still matters in a healthy compost system

Final Thoughts

Composting cardboard and paper safely is a practical way to reduce waste and improve your compost pile at the same time. Plain cardboard, newspaper, paper towels, and other simple paper products can be excellent brown materials when shredded, mixed well, and used in moderation.

The key is to stick with uncoated, non-glossy materials and remove anything synthetic before composting. Done properly, cardboard and paper can help create a more balanced, cleaner, and more effective compost system.
